---
title: "EDA on mtcars dataset"
author: "Natasha Gicheha"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

```{r}
#Load dataset
data(mtcars) #Inbuilt

#Part A: Understanding the Dataset
#1a) Display the first six observations.
head(mtcars)

#b) Display the last six observations.
tail(mtcars)

#c) Determine the number of observations and variables.
# Order is (number of rows, number of columns)
dim(mtcars) #32 rows, 11 columns

#d) Names of all variables
names(mtcars) #Each name is put in quotes

#e) Display the structure of the dataset
#Output:Data type of the dataset
#Data type of each column
#Total number of observations and total variables
#Some observations in each variable
str(mtcars)

#2a) The data type of each variable.
str(mtcars) #all are numerical, data types are written in short form

#b) Which variables should be treated as categorical rather than numerical?
#cyl, gear, vs, am and carb (5)
#Although they are stored as numbers, the values represent groups or categories rather than measurements on a continuous scale.

#c) Convert the appropriate variables into factors.
mtcars$cyl  <- factor(mtcars$cyl)
mtcars$vs   <- factor(mtcars$vs)
mtcars$am   <- factor(mtcars$am)
mtcars$gear <- factor(mtcars$gear)
mtcars$carb <- factor(mtcars$carb)

# Verify the changes
str(mtcars)
    

#Question 3 Perform a quality assessment of the dataset.
#a) Check for missing values.
colSums(is.na(mtcars))#No missing values

#b) Check for duplicated observations.
sum(duplicated(mtcars)) #No duplicates

#c) Determine the number of unique values in each variable.
#unique values=different values that appear in a variable
#not the same as outliers
#function(x) "For every column in mtcars, count the number of unique values.
sapply(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x)))#No of unique values

#d) Produce a summary of all variables.
#Numerical
#Factor datatype frequency of each category.
#cyl variable:
#14 cars (43.75%) have 8 cylinders.
#11 cars (34.38%) have 4 cylinders.
#7 cars (21.88%) have 6 cylinders.
summary(mtcars)

#Part C: Univariate Analysis
#Question 4 Conduct a univariate analysis for the variable Miles per Gallon (mpg).
#a) Compute the mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation.
summary(mtcars$mpg) #mean, median

# Mode
mode_func <- function(x) {
  ux <- unique(x)
  ux[which.max(tabulate(match(x, ux)))]
}

mode_func(mtcars$mpg)

# Variance
var(mtcars$mpg)

# Standard Deviation
sd(mtcars$mpg)

#b) Calculate the minimum, maximum, range, and interquartile range.
summary(mtcars$mpg) #minimum, maximum

# Range
# overall spread.
range(mtcars$mpg)

# Interquartile Range
#spread of the middle 50% of observations
IQR(mtcars$mpg)

#c) Interpret the results.
#1.Mean
#Average fuel efficiency of all vehicles.

#2Median
#Middle mpg value when arranged in order.
#Mode
#Most frequently occurring mpg value.
#Variance
#Measures variability around the mean.
#Standard Deviation
#Average spread of mpg values around the mean.
#Large SD = more variation among vehicles.
#•	Minimum = lowest fuel efficiency.
#•	Maximum = highest fuel efficiency.
#•	Range = overall spread.
#•	IQR = spread of the middle 50% of observations.

#Question 5 Create suitable visualizations for the variable mpg
#a) Histogram
library(ggplot2)
hist(mtcars$mpg,
     main = "Histogram of MPG",
     xlab = "Miles per Gallon",
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black")

#Interpretation
#Right skewed
#Look for:Symmetry, Skewness andPeaks (modes)
#If the bars are concentrated on one side with a long tail, the distribution is skewed.

#b) Boxplot
boxplot(mtcars$mpg,
        main = "Boxplot of MPG",
        ylab = "Miles per Gallon",
        col = "lightgreen")

#Interpretation
#The boxplot shows:
# •	Median (center line) 20
#•	Q1 15 and Q3 ~ 24 (box)
#•	Minimum and maximum values
#•	Outliers (points outside whiskers)
#Potential outliers appear as dots beyond whiskers.

#Question 6 Using all numerical variables,
#numerical variables: 
colnames(mtcars)
#drat, am, wt, disp, qsec, hp, vs

#a) Produce histograms - numeric data types only.
str(mtcars)
# drat variable
hist(mtcars$drat,
     main = "Histogram of DRAT",
     xlab = "DRAT",
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black")


# wt variable
hist(mtcars$wt,
     main = "Histogram of WT",
     xlab = "Weight",
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black")

# disp variable
hist(mtcars$disp,
     main = "Histogram of DISP",
     xlab = "Displacement",
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black")

# qsec variable
hist(mtcars$qsec,
     main = "Histogram of QSEC",
     xlab = "1/4 Mile Time",
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black")

# hp variable
hist(mtcars$hp,
     main = "Histogram of HP",
     xlab = "Horsepower",
     col = "skyblue",
     border = "black")



#Next class presentation!
#4 histograms in one Cartesian plane

#b) Produce boxplots.
# drat variable
boxplot(mtcars$drat,
        main = "Boxplot of DRAT",
        col = "lightgreen")

# wt variable
boxplot(mtcars$wt,
        main = "Boxplot of WT",
        col = "lightgreen")

# disp variable
boxplot(mtcars$disp,
        main = "Boxplot of DISP",
        col = "lightgreen")

# qsec variable
boxplot(mtcars$qsec,
        main = "Boxplot of QSEC",
        col = "lightgreen")

# hp variable
boxplot(mtcars$hp,
        main = "Boxplot of HP",
        col = "lightgreen")

#c) Identify variables with skewed distributions.
#d) Identify variables containing potential outliers.

#9, 10not f

```
